title: 1 Corinthians 11
subtitle: Proper Worship: Head Coverings and the Lord's Supper

sections.header: Verses 1–16 — "Head Coverings and the Order of Creation"

heb.paragraph: The noun kephalē (head) is the most contested term in the chapter, appearing nine times in vv. 3–10. In classical Greek it primarily denotes the physical head; metaphorically it can mean 'source' (the head of a river) or 'authority over.' The debate centres on whether Paul uses kephalē to mean 'authority' (as in most English translations) or 'source/origin' (as argued by many egalitarian scholars citing the parallel with God as 'head' of Christ in v. 3). Fee and Thiselton take different positions on this exegetical crux, making it a productive site for scholarly dialogue.

In v. 10, Paul states that 'a woman ought to have authority (exousia) over her own head, because of the angels.' This verse is notoriously difficult. The most likely reading is that the head covering is a sign of the woman's own authority to pray and prophesy in the assembly—not a badge of subordination but an emblem of empowerment. The reference to 'angels' may reflect the Jewish tradition of angelic presence in worship (cf. Qumran texts 1QSa 2.3–9; 1QM 7.6).

panels.ctx: The head-covering passage (11:2–16) addresses worship practice in the Corinthian assembly. In the Graeco-Roman world, conventions around head coverings varied by region and context. Roman men typically covered their heads during prayer (capite velato), while Greek men did not. For women, a head covering was a near-universal mark of respectability; an uncovered head could signal sexual availability or cultic ecstasy. Paul's argument weaves together creation theology (Gen 1–2), christological hierarchy (v. 3), cultural propriety (vv. 13–15), and church practice (v. 16). The passage presupposes that women pray and prophesy in the assembly (vv. 5, 13)—a point of tension with 14:34–35 that has generated centuries of scholarly discussion.

cross.note: Paul's appeal to the creation of humanity in God's image (v. 7) draws on the Genesis 1 account. The statement that 'man is the image and glory of God; but woman is the glory of man' (v. 7) is Paul's interpretive synthesis of Genesis 1 and 2, not a direct citation.

The creation of woman from man (vv. 8–9) appeals to the Genesis 2 narrative of Eve's formation from Adam's rib. Paul uses this not to subordinate women but to establish the mutuality articulated in vv. 11–12: 'in the Lord woman is not independent of man, nor is man independent of woman.'

The Ephesians passage develops the head/body metaphor in the context of marriage. Both passages use kephalē to describe a relational structure, though the specific application differs (worship practice in 1 Cor 11; marriage in Eph 5).

notes.note: Verse 1 properly concludes the preceding argument of ch. 10: 'Follow my example, as I follow the example of Christ.' The new section begins with v. 2, where Paul praises the Corinthians for maintaining the traditions (paradoseis) he delivered. The hierarchical chain of v. 3—God → Christ → man → woman—establishes the theological framework: the term 'head' (kephalē) denotes both authority and source within a relational order that is not a chain of command but a structure of mutual dependence.

The practical application is straightforward: men should pray and prophesy with uncovered heads; women should do so with covered heads. The rationale involves both creation theology (vv. 7–9) and the enigmatic reference to angels (v. 10). The most likely interpretation is that angels attend the worshipping assembly and expect proper order. The covering functions as a sign of the woman's authority (exousia) to participate in prophetic ministry.

Paul balances the creation argument with a strong statement of mutual dependence: 'In the Lord' neither sex is independent of the other (vv. 11–12). The appeal to 'nature itself' (v. 14) is an argument from cultural convention, not from biology per se. The closing statement—'we have no other practice, nor do the churches of God' (v. 16)—invokes apostolic and ecclesial consensus.

Calvin interprets the head-covering instructions as pertaining to public worship decorum rather than establishing a universal dress code. The principle is order: God has established a relational structure in creation that worship practices should reflect rather than obscure. Calvin is careful to distinguish the universal principle (orderly worship that honours God's design) from its culturally variable expression (specific garments or hairstyles). He notes that Paul's own argument from 'nature' (v. 14) demonstrates that some elements of the instruction are rooted in convention rather than divine command, while the underlying principle of honouring God's order is permanent.

The NET note on kephalē provides an extensive discussion of the 'authority' vs. 'source' debate. The note favours 'authority over' as the primary meaning in this context, noting that the hierarchical chain (God→Christ→man→woman) requires a consistent relational sense. However, the note acknowledges that 'source' cannot be entirely excluded, especially given the creation argument in vv. 8–9.

The NET note addresses the crux interpretum: 'a woman ought to have authority (exousia) on her head.' The note explains that exousia is active ('a sign of her own authority') rather than passive ('a sign of being under authority'), following the standard Pauline usage where exousia refers to a right or power the subject possesses.

Fee, writing from a Pentecostal-egalitarian perspective, argues that kephalē in v. 3 means 'source' rather than 'authority over.' The relational chain is thus one of origin (God is the source of Christ, Christ is the source of man, man is the source of woman via Gen 2), not a hierarchy of command. Fee contends that Paul's primary concern is not gender subordination but gender differentiation: in the new-creation community, the distinctions of male and female are not erased but honoured in worship. The head covering signals a woman's respected participation in prayer and prophecy, not her subjection. Fee reads v. 10 as affirming the woman's own authority (exousia) to prophesy, with the covering serving as a cultural marker of that empowerment.

Thiselton provides the most comprehensive analysis of the passage in modern English commentary. He argues that the term kephalē in v. 3 is used with different nuances in each of its three occurrences: 'source' when predicated of God and Christ, 'preeminent partner' when predicated of Christ and man, and 'source/origin' when predicated of man and woman. Thiselton contends that flattening all three to a single meaning (whether 'authority' or 'source') impoverishes the argument. On the vexed question of v. 10, Thiselton adopts the active reading: the woman's head covering is a sign of her own exousia—her right and freedom to participate in prophetic speech. The reference to angels reflects the Qumran-attested tradition of angelic presence in worship, where proper order is maintained out of respect for the heavenly assembly.

heb.paragraph: The noun schisma (division, tear) echoes 1:10, where Paul first addressed the Corinthian divisions. In a textile metaphor, a schisma is a rip in the fabric of community. The term reappears here because the Lord's Supper, which should be the supreme expression of unity ('one body,' 10:17), has become an occasion for social stratification. The divisions are not theological but socio-economic: the wealthy eat well while the poor go hungry.

The noun haireseis (factions) in v. 19 intensifies the charge beyond schisma. In classical Greek, hairesis denotes a 'choice' or 'school of thought.' Paul's use carries negative connotations: the factions are not legitimate theological differences but sinful divisions that 'show which of you have God's approval'—an ironic statement, since the factions themselves are evidence of disapproval.

panels.ctx: The Corinthian communal meal appears to have been a full dinner (deipnon) in which the Lord's Supper was embedded. Archaeological and social-historical evidence suggests that wealthy patrons hosted the meal in their homes, where the dining room (triclinium) held only nine to twelve reclining guests, while others ate in the atrium or courtyard. The wealthy arrived early (since they controlled their own schedules) and ate the best food and wine, while working-class members arrived later to find the provisions depleted. Paul's outrage is not merely social but theological: this behaviour 'despises the church of God' and 'humiliates those who have nothing' (v. 22).

cross.note: Paul's earlier appeal for unity—'that there be no divisions (schismata) among you'—is directly echoed by the division language of 11:18. The Lord's Supper abuses are a specific instance of the broader Corinthian problem of factionalism.
James rebukes the practice of showing favouritism to the rich in the assembly while marginalising the poor—a close parallel to the Corinthian practice Paul denounces.

notes.note: Paul moves from praise (v. 2) to blame (v. 17): 'Your meetings do more harm than good.' The divisions at the communal meal are not incidental but indicative of a deeper spiritual malaise. Verse 19's 'there have to be differences among you to show which of you have God's approval' is likely ironic: the factions serve to expose who is genuinely committed to the gospel's levelling ethic and who is perpetuating the world's social hierarchies.

Paul's indictment is devastating: 'When you come together, it is not the Lord's Supper you eat' (v. 20). The rich who gorge themselves while the poor go hungry have turned the covenant meal into a private dinner party. 'Don't you have homes to eat and drink in?' (v. 22) is sharp sarcasm: if they want a private meal, they should eat at home and leave the Lord's Supper for what it is—a communal confession of unity in Christ.

Calvin reads this passage as a severe warning against profaning the sacrament through social injustice. The Lord's Supper was instituted as a meal of equality: all partake of the same bread and cup regardless of status. When the rich monopolise the provisions and humiliate the poor, they contradict the very meaning of the rite. Calvin notes that Paul's criticism is not merely about etiquette but about the theological integrity of the church: a community that tolerates such inequality at the Lord's Table has not understood the cross.

The NET note discusses whether Paul's statement is (a) genuine belief that factions have a divine purpose (to distinguish the approved), (b) ironic concession (factions are inevitable given human sin, and at least they reveal character), or (c) prophetic sarcasm. The NET translators lean toward ironic concession, noting the phrase 'no doubt' (dei) which introduces divine necessity.

Fee argues that the abuse Paul addresses is not a theological misunderstanding of the eucharist but a social-ethical failure: the Corinthians have imported Roman patronage conventions into the communal meal. The wealthy treat the gathering as their private dinner (idion deipnon, v. 21), eating ahead of others and consuming the best food. Fee emphasises that Paul's response is not merely to correct table manners but to reassert the Lord's Supper as an eschatological meal that anticipates the kingdom's radical equality.

Thiselton draws on the social-scientific work of Gerd Theissen to reconstruct the socio-economic dynamics behind the abuse. The triclinium-atrium division meant that high-status members reclined at table in the dining room while lower-status members stood or sat in the open courtyard. Thiselton argues that the phrase 'each one goes ahead with his own supper' (v. 21) reflects the Roman convivial practice of bringing differential provisions based on social rank—a practice widely attested in Roman satirical literature (Martial, Juvenal, Pliny). Paul's response shatters this social convention: the Lord's Supper must be eaten 'together' (ekdechesthe allēlous, v. 33).

heb.paragraph: The noun anamnēsis (remembrance) in vv. 24–25 is more than mental recollection. In the LXX and Jewish liturgical tradition, anamnēsis denotes a cultic act that makes a past event present and effective for the current generation (cf. the Passover haggadah). The Lord's Supper does not merely recall Christ's death as a historical fact; it re-presents its saving significance in the gathered community. This understanding bridges Catholic and Protestant eucharistic theology: the event is not repeated (contra transubstantiation as commonly caricatured) but its benefits are made present and effective.

The noun diathēkē (covenant) in v. 25 ('this cup is the new covenant in my blood') links the Lord's Supper to Jeremiah's prophecy of a 'new covenant' (Jer 31:31–34). In Greek, diathēkē can mean either 'covenant' (bilateral agreement) or 'testament/will' (unilateral disposition). The LXX consistently uses it for Hebrew berith (covenant). Jesus' words identify His shed blood as the ratification of the new covenant promised by the prophets—a covenant written on hearts, not on stone tablets.

panels.ctx: Paul's institution narrative (vv. 23–26) is the earliest written account of the Lord's Supper, predating even the Gospel accounts (Mark's Gospel is usually dated to the late 60s AD, while 1 Corinthians was written c. AD 53–55). Paul explicitly claims that he 'received from the Lord' (parelabon apo tou kyriou) what he 'passed on' (paredōka)—the technical vocabulary of Jewish tradition transmission (cf. 15:3). The tradition includes four elements: (1) the bread-word ('This is my body, which is for you'), (2) the command of remembrance, (3) the cup-word ('This cup is the new covenant in my blood'), and (4) the eschatological horizon ('until he comes'). Paul then applies this tradition to the Corinthian situation: unworthy participation brings judgment, including physical illness and death (vv. 29–30).

cross.note: Moses ratified the Sinai covenant by sprinkling blood on the people, saying 'This is the blood of the covenant.' Jesus' cup-word deliberately echoes and transforms this: the new covenant is ratified by His own blood, not the blood of animals.

The Synoptic institution narratives (Matt 26; Mark 14; Luke 22) provide parallel accounts. Paul's version is closest to Luke's, sharing the distinctive phrases 'which is for you' and 'do this in remembrance of me' for both elements.

Luke's institution narrative closely parallels Paul's, both including the command 'Do this in remembrance of me' and the phrase 'new covenant in my blood.' This suggests a shared liturgical tradition underlying both accounts.

notes.note: Paul's institution narrative is the earliest written record of the Last Supper tradition. The phrase 'received from the Lord' (v. 23) denotes authoritative tradition: Paul received this from the Lord via the chain of apostolic witness. The bread-word ('This is my body, which is for you') and the cup-word ('This cup is the new covenant in my blood') are covenantal declarations: Christ's body and blood ratify the new covenant prophesied in Jeremiah 31. The repeated command 'Do this in remembrance of me' (vv. 24–25) makes the supper an ongoing observance. The eschatological note—'until he comes' (v. 26)—frames the supper as a proclamation of the gospel between the two advents.

The warning against 'unworthy manner' (anaxiōs, v. 27) is often misread as referring to personal unworthiness. Paul's concern is the manner of eating, not the moral state of the individual. Eating 'without discerning the body' (v. 29) means failing to recognise the church as Christ's body—precisely the sin of the rich who eat without regard for the poor. The consequences are severe: weakness, sickness, and death (v. 30) are disciplinary judgments within the covenant community.

Calvin regards this passage as the definitive scriptural warrant for the Lord's Supper. The words 'This is my body' are to be understood sacramentally: the bread is not transformed into Christ's physical body (contra Rome) nor is it a bare symbol (contra Zwingli); rather, by the Spirit's power the bread is the instrument through which believers truly receive Christ's body. Calvin emphasises the covenantal dimension: 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood' means that the Supper seals the covenant promises to each believing participant.

Calvin insists that 'discerning the body' (v. 29) has a dual reference: discerning Christ's body offered in the sacrament and discerning the church as Christ's body. Both failures are present in Corinth: the rich neither honour the sacramental significance of the meal nor respect the communal dignity of their fellow members. Calvin reads the disciplinary judgments (v. 30) as fatherly chastisement, not punitive condemnation: 'when we are judged by the Lord, we are being disciplined so that we will not be finally condemned with the world' (v. 32).

The NET note discusses the phrase 'received from the Lord' (parelabon apo tou kyriou). The preposition apo can indicate ultimate source ('from the Lord' = divinely authorised) or mediate transmission ('from the Lord' via the apostolic chain). The NET translators favour the reading that Paul received the tradition ultimately from the Lord through the believing community, not via direct revelation.

The NET note on 'without discerning the body' addresses the textual variant: some manuscripts add 'of the Lord' (tou kyriou), making the phrase 'body of the Lord.' The shorter reading ('the body') is likely original. The question is whether 'body' refers to Christ's physical body (present in the sacrament) or to the church (Christ's corporate body). The NET note argues for the ecclesial reading based on context (the abuse involves mistreating fellow members).

Fee treats the institution narrative as a pre-Pauline liturgical formula that Paul received from the earliest community and passed on intact. He notes the close verbal parallels with Luke 22:19–20 and argues that both derive from a common Antiochene tradition. Fee emphasises the eschatological horizon of v. 26: the Lord's Supper is not merely retrospective (remembering the cross) but prospective (proclaiming 'until he comes'). The meal enacts the already/not-yet tension of the entire Christian life.

Fee reads 'discerning the body' (v. 29) as referring to the church, not to the eucharistic elements. The Corinthians' sin is not a deficient sacramental theology but a deficient ecclesiology: they fail to recognise that the gathered community is Christ's body, and treating fellow members with contempt is tantamount to sinning against Christ himself. The physical consequences (v. 30) are not magical but covenantal: God disciplines His people to prevent their ultimate condemnation (v. 32).

Thiselton provides the most detailed tradition-critical analysis of the institution narrative in any modern commentary. He identifies the formula as deriving from the earliest Antiochene community (c. AD 35–40) and notes its 'fixed' character: the words have a liturgical stability that resists Pauline paraphrase. Thiselton argues that anamnēsis (remembrance) should be understood against the background of Jewish memorial sacrifice: the Passover haggadah commands the participants to 'regard themselves as if they had come out of Egypt.' Similarly, the Lord's Supper makes the cross event present for each generation of believers.

Thiselton argues that the phrase 'in an unworthy manner' (anaxiōs) is an adverb modifying the act of eating, not an adjective describing the eater. The Corinthians are not unworthy people; they are eating in an unworthy way—namely, by perpetuating social stratification at the very meal that proclaims the cross's abolition of human distinctions. Thiselton reads v. 30's references to illness and death as Paul's interpretation of actual events in the Corinthian community: some members have become sick or died, and Paul attributes this to divine discipline.

themes.note: Worship dominates as Paul addresses head coverings and the Lord’s Supper. Covenant and Judgment score highly due to the new-covenant institution narrative and the warnings about unworthy participation.

rows.label: vv.2–16
rows.text: Head coverings: creation theology, christological hierarchy, cultural propriety

rows.label: vv.17–22
rows.text: Lord's Supper abuses: socio-economic divisions at the communal meal

rows.label: vv.23–26
rows.text: Institution narrative: earliest written account of the Last Supper tradition

rows.label: vv.27–34
rows.text: Application: self-examination, discerning the body, divine discipline

lit.note: Chapter 11 addresses two worship disorders: head coverings (vv. 2–16) and Lord’s Supper abuses (vv. 17–34). Both sections share a common concern: worship practices must reflect the theological realities they signify.

hebtext.note: The noun kephalē (head) is the most contested term in the chapter, appearing nine times in vv. 3–10. In classical Greek it primarily denotes the physical head; metaphorically it can mean 'source' (the head of a river) or 'authority over.' The debate centres on whether Paul uses kephalē to mean 'authority' (as in most English translations) or 'source/origin' (as argued by many egalitarian scholars citing the parallel with God as 'head' of Christ in v. 3). Fee and Thiselton take different positions on this exegetical crux, making it a productive site for scholarly dialogue.

In v. 10, Paul states that 'a woman ought to have authority (exousia) over her own head, because of the angels.' This verse is notoriously difficult. The most likely reading is that the head covering is a sign of the woman's own authority to pray and prophesy in the assembly—not a badge of subordination but an emblem of empowerment. The reference to 'angels' may reflect the Jewish tradition of angelic presence in worship (cf. Qumran texts 1QSa 2.3–9; 1QM 7.6).

The noun schisma (division, tear) echoes 1:10, where Paul first addressed the Corinthian divisions. In a textile metaphor, a schisma is a rip in the fabric of community. The term reappears here because the Lord's Supper, which should be the supreme expression of unity ('one body,' 10:17), has become an occasion for social stratification. The divisions are not theological but socio-economic: the wealthy eat well while the poor go hungry.

The noun haireseis (factions) in v. 19 intensifies the charge beyond schisma. In classical Greek, hairesis denotes a 'choice' or 'school of thought.' Paul's use carries negative connotations: the factions are not legitimate theological differences but sinful divisions that 'show which of you have God's approval'—an ironic statement, since the factions themselves are evidence of disapproval.

The noun anamnēsis (remembrance) in vv. 24–25 is more than mental recollection. In the LXX and Jewish liturgical tradition, anamnēsis denotes a cultic act that makes a past event present and effective for the current generation (cf. the Passover haggadah). This understanding bridges Catholic and Protestant eucharistic theology: the event is not repeated (contra transubstantiation as commonly caricatured) but its benefits are made present and effective.

The noun diathēkē (covenant) in v. 25 ('this cup is the new covenant in my blood') links the Lord's Supper to Jeremiah's prophecy of a 'new covenant' (Jer 31:31–34). In Greek, diathēkē can mean either 'covenant' (bilateral agreement) or 'testament/will' (unilateral disposition). The LXX consistently uses it for Hebrew berith (covenant). Jesus' words identify His shed blood as the ratification of the new covenant promised by the prophets—a covenant written on hearts, not on stone tablets.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: 10: 'Follow my example, as I follow the example of Christ…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Proper Worship: Head Coverings and the Lord's Supper

translations.text: For if a woman does not cover her head, she might as well have her hair cut off; but if it is a disgrace for a woman to have her hair cut off or her head shaved, then she should cover her head.
For if a wife will not cover her head, then she should cut her hair short. But since it is disgraceful for a wife to cut off her hair or shave her head, let her cover her head.
Follow my example, as I follow the example of Christ.
Be imitators of me, as I am of Christ.
